title: Defense Aviation Has a Readiness Problem. AI Can Help Fix It.
gist: Defense aviation fleets across the US, France, Germany, and UK suffer chronically low mission-capable rates despite rising spending; BCG argues the fix is not more money but AI-enabled maintenance and sustainment across GenAI maintainer copilots, supply-chain control towers, and predictive/proactive sustainment.

insights.claim: Defense aviation mission-capable rates are routinely below 70% in the US, France, and Germany, and only one-third of the UK's F-35 fleet was fully mission capable in 2024.
insights.speaker: Lacy Ketzner
insights.so_what: Persistently low readiness signals a structural sustainment problem in Western air forces, a demand pool for maintenance-software and predictive-analytics vendors rather than for more airframes or spares spending.
insights.quote: In the US, France, and Germany, mission-capable rates are routinely below 70%. In the UK, only one-third of the UK’s F-35 fleet was fully mission capable in 2024.
insights.locator: The nature of conflict worldwide is changing

insights.claim: Low readiness has persisted despite growing investment in maintainers, spare parts, and depot capacity, implying the problem is process design rather than under-funding.
insights.speaker: Lacy Ketzner
insights.so_what: Reframes the spending thesis: the marginal defense dollar for readiness may flow to software and process redesign, not hardware, capping upside for spares/depot suppliers and favoring AI enablers.
insights.quote: The solution isn’t to spend more, it’s to rethink maintenance and sustainment processes, increasingly through the use of AI.
insights.locator: These levels persist despite growing investments

insights.claim: A retiring, long-tenured maintainer workforce plus fragmented, often paper-based documentation makes on-site troubleshooting slow and constrains fleet readiness.
insights.speaker: Lacy Ketzner
insights.so_what: The workforce/knowledge-transfer gap is the bottleneck GenAI targets; vendors that can ingest legacy and paper technical docs into a usable copilot address a concrete, defensible pain point.
insights.quote: much of the documentation for aviation maintenance is spread across a range of disparate sources—including technical documents that are still often on paper

insights.claim: GenAI copilots can synthesize scattered technical information into an accessible format, letting less-experienced maintainers identify the correct repair process plus required tools and parts and complete jobs on the first pass.
insights.speaker: Lacy Ketzner
insights.so_what: First-pass completion and faster onboarding are the measurable KPIs to track for any GenAI maintenance deployment; watch pilots for hard throughput gains before crediting the thesis.
insights.locator: GenAI can compile and synthesize this information

insights.claim: Spare-parts constraints are often a visibility problem rather than a true shortage—needed parts exist but the service cannot locate them and route them to the right aircraft at the right time.
insights.speaker: Lacy Ketzner
insights.so_what: If shortages are largely visibility-driven, AI supply control towers can lift readiness without added inventory spend, favoring logistics-software plays over parts stockpiling.
insights.quote: Needed parts sit on a pallet somewhere, but the service can’t accurately locate it and get it to the right maintainer on the right aircraft at the right time.

insights.claim: Predictive-maintenance solutions let squadrons anticipate repairs and pre-stage parts, toolkits, and maintainers before an aircraft is downed, shifting sustainment from reactive to proactive.
insights.speaker: Lacy Ketzner
insights.so_what: Predictive maintenance moves the readiness lever upstream; the investable question is which vendors have the fleet sensor/operational data access to make forecasts accurate.
insights.locator: Shift from Reactive to Proactive Sustainment

insights.claim: AI-driven reliability control boards can pinpoint the small subset of long-tail parts and processes that drive a disproportionate share of down-aircraft days, then push suppliers or OEMs on production and design changes.
insights.speaker: Lacy Ketzner
insights.so_what: A Pareto lens on down-aircraft days points procurement pressure at specific OEM parts and could reshape aftermarket demand and future variant designs.
insights.locator: Reliability control boards can help as well

insights.claim: Boards can use real-world operational data to cull mandatory inspections and processes that persist only because of outdated requirements, freeing capacity in the sustainment system.
insights.speaker: Lacy Ketzner
insights.so_what: Data-driven retirement of unnecessary inspections is a readiness gain that costs nothing extra—a soft catalyst to watch in fleet sustainment reforms.
insights.locator: Boards can also look at mandatory inspections
